questions.question: Explain the basic architecture and training objective of a vanilla autoencoder.
keyPoints: Encoder maps input x to latent representation h = f(Wx + b)
Decoder reconstructs x' = g(W' h + b')
Objective: minimize reconstruction loss (e.g., MSE) between x and x'
Bottleneck forces a compressed representation; undercomplete autoencoders learn useful features

questions.question: Describe the differences between denoising autoencoder (DAE), sparse autoencoder (SAE), and contractive autoencoder (CAE) in terms of their regularization mechanisms and training objectives.
keyPoints: DAE reconstructs clean input from a corrupted version, forcing learning of robust features
SAE adds a sparsity penalty (L1 or KL) on hidden activations to encourage disentangled representations
CAE adds a Frobenius‑norm penalty on the Jacobian of the hidden representation w.r.t. input, improving robustness
All three aim to avoid trivial identity mapping and improve feature extraction

questions.question: Explain the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) model, including the probabilistic encoder‑decoder formulation, the loss function (reconstruction loss + KL divergence), the reparameterization trick, and its applications in generative modeling.
keyPoints: Probabilistic encoder outputs mean μ(z|x) and variance σ²(z|x); decoder models p(x|z)
Loss = E[log p(x|z)] – KL(q(z|x) || p(z)); reconstruction term + KL regularization
Reparameterization trick: z = μ + σ⊙ε, ε∼N(0,I) to make sampling differentiable
Used for generating new samples, interpolating in latent space, semi‑supervised learning, and anomaly detection

questions.question: Discuss practical aspects of designing, training, and evaluating autoencoders. Include design choices (bottleneck size, activation functions), training tips (learning rate, early stopping, regularization), evaluation metrics (reconstruction error, latent‑space visualization), and real‑world applications (anomaly detection, image denoising, recommendation systems). Provide a brief pseudo‑code or outline of a PyTorch implementation.
keyPoints: Design: choose bottleneck dimension < input, use ReLU/tanh, symmetric encoder‑decoder architecture
Training: Adam optimizer, monitor validation loss, apply dropout/L2, use early stopping to avoid over‑fitting
Evaluation: compute MSE or binary cross‑entropy on test set, visualize latent space with t‑SNE/UMAP, analyze reconstruction error distribution for anomalies
Applications: detect anomalies via high reconstruction error, denoise images, build collaborative‑filtering recommender systems
